Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to carve out about 15 hours a week, usually in chunks between my work shifts and other responsibilities. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. I had good intentions about being consistent, but life kept getting in the way, and I found myself cramming more than I'd like to admit.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be really disciplined and study at the same times, but then a deadline would hit or something would come up at work, and everything would shift. I'm 27, so I've got more competing demands than maybe a younger student would have. I tried to be regular but honestly, I was more reactive than proactive most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or wanting to check if there was new content posted. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post and that would pull me in. I'd also log in when I had a specific question or needed to download materials for offline studying. It wasn't always a daily habit—more like when I had a specific purpose or felt guilty about not checking in for a while.
Q4: There were a few weeks early in the semester where checking the LMS became more automatic for me. I'd wake up, have my coffee, and scroll through what was new. But that routine fell apart when things got stressful at work and I started feeling overwhelmed by how many courses I was juggling. The CBT sessions I did helped me recognize this pattern and try to rebuild some consistency, but it was a struggle to maintain.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because of how I learn best through listening. I watched over two hours of video content and would often replay sections multiple times. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click for me. When someone explained something in a different way in the forums, it was like having a study group I could access anytime.
Q6: I'll be honest—I often skipped the reading materials, especially the dense PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but then never get through them. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd just skim or ignore them entirely. Time was always tight, so I had to prioritize what gave me the most learning for the time invested.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could listen to it or if it involved some interaction with other students, I was more likely to engage deeply. I also valued activities that gave me feedback quickly so I knew if I was on the right track. Anything that felt like busywork or didn't clearly build toward something got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With 19 courses to manage and limited time, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted. Optional ones I'd look at and think 'that would be nice' but then something urgent would come up and they'd disappear from my radar entirely.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture audio and listen on my phone during my commute or while doing housework. I also had a notebook where I'd write out concepts by hand after listening to lectures. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or podcasts that explained topics in ways that clicked better for me. The LMS was more of a starting point than where all my learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-listening to downloaded lectures, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself—sounds weird, but it really helps me as an auditory learner. Some of my best studying happened on the bus with my earbuds in, just processing what I'd heard earlier. The LMS would show nothing for those hours.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or skim it once online, and then continue working with it offline for days. My assignment scores were decent at 74% because I actually engaged deeply with the material, just not always in ways the system could track. I'd come back to the same downloaded lecture three or four times before I felt I really understood it.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was dealing with moderate stress and anxiety, and my attendance dropped significantly because I just couldn't keep up with everything. Work was demanding more hours, and I felt like I was drowning in all 19 courses. The CBT sessions helped me develop some coping strategies, but there were definitely weeks where I barely engaged at all because I was just trying to survive.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about course requirements made me avoid certain sections entirely because I didn't know where to start. My competing responsibilities—work, personal stuff—meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of energy I had left. Some days that was genuine engagement, other days it was just going through the motions.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered to my to-do list or worries about other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. My engagement level might have looked high on paper, but the quality of that engagement varied wildly depending on my mental state that day.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was anxiety—if I didn't start, I couldn't fail, right? The CBT sessions helped me recognize this avoidance pattern. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this, even though when I did complete work, I usually did reasonably well on it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, for better or worse. I'd have low activity for days, then suddenly log in multiple times as a deadline approached. My response time averaged over 11 hours, which shows I wasn't always quick to react. Deadlines created stress but also forced me to prioritize. Without them, I probably would have engaged even less consistently than I did.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But feedback was sometimes slow or minimal, which made it hard to know if I was on track. After getting a disappointing exam score, I felt deflated and actually engaged less for a while. The CBT sessions helped me reframe feedback as information rather than judgment, which made it easier to keep going.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with so much content spread across 19 courses. When things were clearly organized and I knew exactly what was expected, I engaged more. But when the structure was confusing or requirements weren't clear, I'd disengage out of frustration. I needed more scaffolding than was provided, especially given everything else I was balancing.
Q19: Having video content was huge for me as an auditory learner—that made engagement much easier. The discussion forums were also accessible and helped me feel connected. What made it harder was when there were too many different types of activities or unclear instructions. Also, the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single one.
Q20: I think that was partly me, honestly. I'd spend a lot of time in the LMS watching videos, participating in discussions, reading posts—but then struggle to actually complete and submit assignments. Sometimes it's easier to consume content than to produce something. Fear of not doing well enough, or just running out of time and energy before getting to the submission part. The engagement is real, but it doesn't always translate to completed work.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some students might be really efficient—they download what they need, study offline, and just submit their work without a lot of LMS activity. Or they might have strong background knowledge and not need as much engagement with course materials. I wish I could have been more like that, but I needed more support and interaction, even if it didn't always show in my final performance.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what resources you access. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether I was actually focused or just had the tab open. They definitely can't show the mental effort, the anxiety, the late nights reviewing downloaded materials. Numbers only tell part of the story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to things I already know or to my life. For me, it often involves listening and talking through concepts, even if just to myself. Real engagement leaves me feeling like I've grown, even if it's exhausting. It's more than just showing up—it's being mentally present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures on the bus, or the anxiety I pushed through to participate in discussions. It can't capture that I'm a 27-year-old balancing multiple responsibilities while trying to improve my life through education. My resilience, my determination to keep climbing even when I stumbled—none of that shows up in the data.
